levels.description: Deployment ghost-deployment exists in the cluster but there are no running pods. Run kubectl get pods -n k8smissions and notice the output is empty.
levels.hint1:
The Deployment was created successfully — Kubernetes accepted it without errors. But zero pods appeared. This means the Deployment is not asking for any pods.

Check the Deployment's current desired state:

  kubectl get deployment ghost-deployment -n k8smissions

Look at the READY and DESIRED columns.
levels.hint2:
The DESIRED column shows 0. Kubernetes is doing exactly what was asked — running zero replicas.

Get the full spec to see where this is set:

  kubectl get deployment ghost-deployment -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep 'replicas'
levels.hint3:
The field `spec.replicas: 0` is why no pods exist. A Deployment with 0 replicas is valid in Kubernetes — it is used to 'park' a workload without deleting it.

Open manifest.yaml and find the replicas field.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

`spec.replicas: 0` tells the Deployment controller to maintain exactly zero running pods. Kubernetes did not make an error — it followed the spec perfectly.

## Fix

Change the replicas count in manifest.yaml:

```yaml
spec:
  replicas: 3
```

## Why this matters

Setting replicas to 0 is a deliberate operation in production — it lets you stop a workload without deleting it or its configuration. When you are ready to restart, you scale back up. If you ever wonder why a Deployment is empty, check replicas before anything else.

levels.description: Deployment web-app was recently updated and all pods are stuck in ImagePullBackOff. The application is down. Run kubectl get pods -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
The image used in the update does not exist. The fastest recovery is not to edit the manifest — it is to roll back to the last working revision that Kubernetes already has recorded.

Check the rollout history:

  kubectl rollout history deployment/web-app -n k8smissions

How many revisions are there?
levels.hint2:
There are two revisions. Revision 1 was the working deployment; revision 2 is the broken one currently running.

See what revision 1 looked like:

  kubectl rollout history deployment/web-app -n k8smissions --revision=1

Note the image and change-cause annotation.
levels.hint3:
Revision 1 used a working image. You can roll back to it without touching manifest.yaml at all:

  kubectl rollout undo deployment/web-app -n k8smissions

After rolling back, update manifest.yaml to match the restored state so the next apply does not re-break it.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The Deployment was updated to `image: nginx:nonexistent-v2.0-xyz` — a tag that does not exist. All pods entered ImagePullBackOff because the registry returned 404.

## Fix

Roll back immediately:

```bash
kubectl rollout undo deployment/web-app -n k8smissions
```

Then verify recovery:

```bash
kubectl rollout status deployment/web-app -n k8smissions
```

Finally update manifest.yaml image to match the working revision:

```yaml
image: nginx:latest
```

## Why this matters

Kubernetes keeps a rollout history (controlled by `revisionHistoryLimit`, default 10). This makes rollback instant — no rebuild, no re-push, just pointer to an old ReplicaSet. In a real incident, `kubectl rollout undo` is often the first command you run.

levels.description: Pods in Deployment api-server keep restarting despite the container starting up cleanly. The RESTARTS column keeps climbing. Run kubectl get pods -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
The container starts successfully, then gets killed shortly after — this is a liveness probe failure pattern, not a CrashLoopBackOff from a bad command.

Get the full picture:

  kubectl describe pod -l app=api -n k8smissions

Scroll to the Events section. Look for 'Liveness probe failed'.
levels.hint2:
Events show 'Liveness probe failed: HTTP probe failed with statuscode: 404'. The probe is hitting an endpoint that does not exist on nginx — nginx returns 404, which the probe treats as failure.

See what path the probe is checking:

  kubectl get deployment api-server -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A8 'livenessProbe'
levels.hint3:
The livenessProbe is configured with `path: /nonexistent-healthz`. Nginx does not serve anything at that path — it returns 404 on every probe check.

The fix is in `spec.template.spec.containers[0].livenessProbe.httpGet.path` in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The liveness probe sends `GET /nonexistent-healthz` to port 80. Nginx does not have a route for that path and returns HTTP 404. Kubernetes treats any non-2xx response as probe failure. After `failureThreshold: 2` consecutive failures, it kills and restarts the container — even though the container itself was perfectly healthy.

## Fix

Change the probe path to one nginx actually serves:

```yaml
livenessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /
    port: 80
  initialDelaySeconds: 5
  periodSeconds: 10
  failureThreshold: 3
```

## Why this matters

A misconfigured liveness probe is one of the nastiest production problems — the application works fine but Kubernetes keeps killing it. Always validate that your probe endpoint returns 200 before deploying. A probe hitting a non-existent path creates an infinite restart loop that is indistinguishable from a real crash at first glance.

levels.description: Deployment slow-startup-app is running and pods show Ready, but users are hitting intermittent 502 errors immediately after a pod starts. Run kubectl get pods -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
502 errors on startup suggest traffic is being sent to pods that have not finished initializing. Check whether a fresh pod is immediately added to the Service endpoints:

  kubectl get endpoints slow-app-service -n k8smissions

Then delete one pod and watch what happens to Endpoints in real time:

  kubectl get endpoints slow-app-service -n k8smissions -w
levels.hint2:
The new pod appears in Endpoints immediately — before it is actually ready to serve. The application runs `sleep 20` before starting nginx, so for the first 20 seconds it cannot respond to requests.

Check whether a readiness probe exists:

  kubectl get deployment slow-startup-app -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A8 'readinessProbe'
levels.hint3:
There is no `readinessProbe` defined. Without one, Kubernetes assumes the pod is ready as soon as all containers are started — not when the application inside is actually ready.

You need to add a `readinessProbe` to spec.template.spec.containers[0] in manifest.yaml. It should account for the 20-second startup delay.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The container runs `sleep 20 && nginx -g 'daemon off;'`. For the first 20 seconds, nothing is listening on port 80. Without a readiness probe, Kubernetes adds the pod to the Service endpoints the moment the container process starts — and traffic immediately starts arriving at a port with nothing behind it.

## Fix

Add a readiness probe that waits until after the sleep:

```yaml
readinessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /
    port: 80
  initialDelaySeconds: 22
  periodSeconds: 5
  successThreshold: 1
```

## Liveness vs Readiness

- **livenessProbe** — is the container alive? If not, kill and restart it.
- **readinessProbe** — is the container ready to receive traffic? If not, remove it from Service endpoints (but do not restart it).

You need both for slow-starting applications.

levels.description: An HPA has been configured for web-backend but it shows <unknown> for current CPU metrics and is not scaling. Run kubectl get hpa -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
The HPA reads CPU metrics from the Kubernetes Metrics API. If it shows <unknown>, that API is not returning data.

Check the HPA's status in detail:

  kubectl describe hpa web-backend-hpa -n k8smissions

Look for the reason it cannot fetch metrics.
levels.hint2:
The HPA says 'unable to get metrics for resource cpu: unable to fetch metrics from resource metrics API'. This means the Metrics Server — the component that collects pod resource usage — is not available.

Check if metrics-server is running:

  kubectl get pods -n k8smissions -l app=metrics-server
levels.hint3:
The metrics-server Deployment exists but has 0 replicas — it is not running. Without it, the Metrics API returns nothing, and the HPA cannot make scaling decisions.

Open manifest.yaml and find the metrics-server Deployment.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The metrics-server Deployment has `replicas: 0`. No metrics-server pod is running, so the Kubernetes Metrics API has no data to serve. The HPA checks that API every 15 seconds and gets nothing — it shows `<unknown>` and makes no scaling decisions.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, change the metrics-server Deployment replicas:

```yaml
replicas: 1
```

## Why this matters

In a real cluster, metrics-server is installed cluster-wide as an add-on (not in the application namespace). Here it is simulated. But the lesson is the same: before creating an HPA, always verify that `kubectl top pods` works — if it does, the Metrics API is available and the HPA will function.

levels.description: A rolling update was triggered on Deployment critical-api and all pods went down at once, causing a complete outage. Run kubectl get pods -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
A rolling update should replace pods one at a time, keeping most of them running. Something in the update strategy allowed all pods to be terminated simultaneously.

Read the Deployment strategy:

  kubectl describe deployment critical-api -n k8smissions

Look at the RollingUpdateStrategy section.
levels.hint2:
The strategy shows `Max Unavailable: 100%`. This means during an update, Kubernetes is permitted to take down all 3 pods at once before starting new ones. With `Max Surge: 0`, no extra pods can be created — so the cluster goes to zero capacity.

This is a valid configuration for a Recreate-style update, but deadly for a service that needs to stay available.
levels.hint3:
The problem is `rollingUpdate.maxUnavailable: 100%` in manifest.yaml. For a zero-downtime rolling update, this value must be low enough to keep pods serving traffic while new ones come up.

Find this field under spec.strategy.rollingUpdate.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

`maxUnavailable: 100%` with `maxSurge: 0` tells Kubernetes: 'you can take down all 3 pods before creating any new ones'. This is essentially the Recreate strategy written in RollingUpdate form. The service had zero running pods during the entire update window.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml:

```yaml
strategy:
  type: RollingUpdate
  rollingUpdate:
    maxUnavailable: 1
    maxSurge: 1
```

## Rule of thumb

- `maxUnavailable: 1` → at most 1 pod down at any time
- `maxSurge: 1` → one extra pod can be created during the update
- Result: 3 replicas always has at least 2 running while new ones are being prepared

levels.description: A node needs to be drained for maintenance but kubectl drain keeps failing. Run kubectl drain <node-name> --ignore-daemonsets and read the error.
levels.hint1:
kubectl drain gracefully evicts pods before draining the node. If it fails, something is blocking eviction. Check if there is a PodDisruptionBudget protecting these pods:

  kubectl get pdb -n k8smissions

Then read its details:

  kubectl describe pdb db-proxy-pdb -n k8smissions
levels.hint2:
The PDB shows `minAvailable: 3` and the Deployment has 3 replicas. This means all 3 pods must be running at all times — but evicting even one pod would temporarily drop the count to 2, violating the budget.

Kubernetes respects the PDB and refuses to evict any pod. The drain cannot proceed.

Check the replica count:

  kubectl get deployment database-proxy -n k8smissions
levels.hint3:
The PDB `minAvailable: 3` equals the total replica count. You need to lower `minAvailable` to a number that allows at least one pod to be evicted.

Find spec.minAvailable in the PodDisruptionBudget in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The PDB sets `minAvailable: 3`, which equals the Deployment's replica count. This means zero pods can ever be voluntarily removed — any eviction attempt (node drain, cluster upgrade, node maintenance) is permanently blocked.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, change the PDB:

```yaml
spec:
  minAvailable: 2
```

This allows 1 pod to be evicted while keeping 2 running — enough for the node drain to proceed.

## Why this matters

PodDisruptionBudgets are a safety mechanism for cluster maintenance. Set `minAvailable` to the minimum number of pods your service can tolerate. A value equal to the replica count means your cluster can never be safely upgraded or maintained — a common mistake when ops and dev teams configure PDBs independently.

levels.description: A new green version of the app has been deployed and all its pods are Running. But users are still seeing the old blue version. Run kubectl get pods -n k8smissions and count how many pods are running per version.
levels.hint1:
Both blue and green Deployments are healthy. The issue is in how the Service decides which pods to send traffic to. Check the Service:

  kubectl describe service app-service -n k8smissions

Look at the Selector section.
levels.hint2:
The Service selector shows `version=blue`. Now check what labels the green pods have:

  kubectl get pods -n k8smissions --show-labels | grep green

Does the Service selector match the green pods?
levels.hint3:
The Service selector is `version: blue` — it only routes to the old Deployment. The green pods exist and are healthy, but no traffic reaches them because the Service ignores them.

Find the selector field inside the Service spec in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The Service selector `version: blue` matches only the old blue Deployment's pods. The green Deployment was deployed but no one updated the Service to point to it. Green pods are running with no traffic.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, under the Service spec:

```yaml
spec:
  selector:
    app: myapp
    version: green
```

## Blue-green deployment pattern

This is the core mechanic of blue-green deploys: both versions run simultaneously, but the Service selector controls which one receives traffic. Switching is instant — one field change. Rolling back is equally instant — switch the selector back to blue. No pod restarts required.

levels.description: A canary deployment is running alongside the stable version. But instead of testing with a small percentage of traffic, about 50% of requests are hitting the experimental canary version. Run kubectl get pods -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
Kubernetes routes traffic proportionally by pod count, not by percentage. If there are 5 stable pods and 5 canary pods, traffic splits 50/50.

Count the pods per version:

  kubectl get pods -n k8smissions -l app=myapp --show-labels
levels.hint2:
Both app-stable and app-canary have 5 replicas. The total is 10 pods. 5 are canary — that is 50% of traffic going to an unvalidated version.

For a proper canary, only 10% of traffic should go to canary. With a total of 10 pods, that means:
- stable: 9 pods
- canary: 1 pod

Check the Deployments:

  kubectl get deployments -n k8smissions
levels.hint3:
Both Deployments need their replica counts adjusted. app-stable should have 9 replicas and app-canary should have 1 replica — giving a 90/10 traffic split.

Find the replicas field for each Deployment in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

Both Deployments have 5 replicas. Since the Service selector matches all pods with `app: myapp`, Kubernetes distributes traffic evenly across all 10 pods — 50% to stable, 50% to canary. A canary should expose only 10% of users to the new version.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml:

```yaml
# app-stable
spec:
  replicas: 9

# app-canary
spec:
  replicas: 1
```

## Why this matters

This is how Kubernetes-native canary deployments work: replica count controls traffic percentage. 1 out of 10 pods = ~10% traffic to canary. Tools like Argo Rollouts or Flagger automate this pattern and add metrics-based promotion/rollback — but the underlying mechanism is the same replica ratio.

levels.description: A database workload is running as a Deployment. After any pod restarts, the pod gets a new random name and a new hostname — breaking application-level replication that depends on stable identities. Run kubectl get pods -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
Compare pod names between restart cycles. Delete one pod and watch what name it comes back with:

  kubectl delete pod <database-pod-name> -n k8smissions
  kubectl get pods -n k8smissions -w

Is the new pod name predictable or random?
levels.hint2:
The replacement pod got a random suffix (like `database-7b9c4-xkz91`). Deployments generate random names for each pod — that is by design. But databases need stable hostnames so replicas can find each other by name.

Check the current resource type:

  kubectl get deployment database -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep 'kind'
levels.hint3:
The resource is a `kind: Deployment`. For stateful workloads that need stable, predictable pod names like `database-0`, `database-1`, `database-2`, you need a `kind: StatefulSet`.

Open manifest.yaml — you need to change the kind and add the required `serviceName` field.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

A Deployment was used for a stateful workload. Deployments treat pods as interchangeable — random names, no ordering guarantees. Databases need stable network identities (`database-0.database-service`) so replicas can discover and connect to each other.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, change the resource:

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: database
  namespace: k8smissions
spec:
  serviceName: "database-service"   # required field for StatefulSet
  replicas: 3
  # ... rest of spec unchanged
```

## StatefulSet guarantees

- Stable pod names: `database-0`, `database-1`, `database-2`
- Stable DNS: `database-0.database-service.k8smissions.svc.cluster.local`
- Ordered startup: pod N does not start until pod N-1 is Running and Ready
- Ordered shutdown: reverse order

levels.description: A new image was applied to Deployment frontend but no new pods are being created. The rollout appears completely frozen — kubectl get pods shows only old pods. Run kubectl rollout status deployment/frontend -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
The rollout status command may say 'Waiting for deployment to finish: 0 out of 3 new replicas have been updated'. This is different from a failed rollout — the Deployment is not progressing at all.

Get more detail:

  kubectl describe deployment frontend -n k8smissions

Look for any mention of 'paused' in the output.
levels.hint2:
The Deployment description shows `Paused: True`. This is a deliberate Deployment feature — you can pause a rollout to apply multiple changes before continuing.

Check the spec directly:

  kubectl get deployment frontend -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep 'paused'
levels.hint3:
The field `spec.paused: true` is set in manifest.yaml. This freezes all rollout activity. Any changes to the pod template are recorded but not applied until the Deployment is resumed.

Find this field in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

`spec.paused: true` is set in the manifest. When a Deployment is paused, the controller records changes to the pod template but does not create any new ReplicaSets or pods. The rollout is frozen indefinitely.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml:

```yaml
spec:
  paused: false
```

## Why this field exists

Pausing is useful when you want to make several changes to a Deployment (update image, change env vars, adjust resources) and only trigger one rollout at the end instead of one per change. The workflow is: pause → apply changes → resume. Leaving a Deployment paused in production is almost always a mistake — but it can be subtle because the Deployment itself looks healthy.

levels.description: The cluster is filling up with pods from CronJob data-processor. Dozens of identical pods are running simultaneously and resource pressure is building. Run kubectl get pods -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
Count how many pods this CronJob has created:

  kubectl get pods -n k8smissions -l job-name

Also check how long each pod has been running:

  kubectl get pods -n k8smissions --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp

What is the relationship between the pod runtime and the CronJob schedule interval?
levels.hint2:
Each pod runs for 120 seconds (the `sleep 120` command). The CronJob fires every 60 seconds. This means a new job starts every minute, but the previous one is still running — and because `concurrencyPolicy: Allow` is set, Kubernetes allows multiple jobs to run at the same time.

Check the CronJob spec:

  kubectl get cronjob data-processor -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep 'concurrencyPolicy'
levels.hint3:
The `concurrencyPolicy: Allow` field lets a new job start even if the previous one is still running. For a job that takes longer than its schedule interval, this creates unbounded accumulation.

Find this field in the CronJob spec in manifest.yaml.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

`concurrencyPolicy: Allow` + a 120-second job on a 60-second schedule = each minute starts a new job while the last one is still running. After 10 minutes there are 10 concurrent pods. After an hour, 60. The cluster eventually runs out of resources.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml:

```yaml
spec:
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
```

## The three concurrencyPolicy values

- `Allow` — run concurrent jobs (the default; fine if jobs are short and idempotent)
- `Forbid` — skip the new run if the previous one is still running
- `Replace` — cancel the previous run and start a new one

For jobs that must not run in parallel (database exports, report generators), always use `Forbid`.

levels.description: After deleting an old Deployment, stale pods are still running in the namespace. The Deployment is gone but the pods refuse to die. Run kubectl get pods -n k8smissions and kubectl get deployments -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
Pods that survive after their parent Deployment is deleted are usually kept alive by a ReplicaSet that was never cleaned up. Check if any ReplicaSets exist:

  kubectl get replicasets -n k8smissions

Is there a ReplicaSet with no owner?
levels.hint2:
There is a ReplicaSet named `legacy-app-7d8f9` with 2 replicas. Check who owns it:

  kubectl get replicaset legacy-app-7d8f9 -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep -A5 'ownerReferences'

If ownerReferences is empty or missing, this ReplicaSet has no parent — it is an orphan.
levels.hint3:
The ReplicaSet has no ownerReferences. It is not controlled by any Deployment, so it will never be garbage collected automatically. It just keeps its pods running.

This level does not require editing manifest.yaml — you need to delete the orphaned ReplicaSet directly.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

When a Deployment is deleted normally, Kubernetes garbage-collects the ReplicaSets it owned and the pods those ReplicaSets managed. But this ReplicaSet has no ownerReferences — either it was created directly (not via a Deployment), or ownership was severed. Without an owner, Kubernetes does not clean it up automatically.

## Fix

```bash
kubectl delete replicaset legacy-app-7d8f9 -n k8smissions
```

Deleting the ReplicaSet also deletes the pods it controls.

## Why this matters

Orphaned ReplicaSets are a common leftover from manual operations or failed migrations. They consume resources silently. `kubectl get replicasets -n <namespace>` should be part of any namespace audit — look for ReplicaSets with no corresponding Deployment.

levels.description: Deployment api-server had its image updated but the rollout is completely stalled — no new pods have been created and nothing has changed. Run kubectl rollout status deployment/api-server -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
The rollout status command hangs or says 'Waiting for rollout to finish'. No pods are being replaced. This is different from a failing rollout — nothing is happening at all.

Read the Deployment update strategy:

  kubectl describe deployment api-server -n k8smissions

Look at the RollingUpdateStrategy section carefully.
levels.hint2:
The strategy shows `Max Unavailable: 0` and `Max Surge: 0`. Think about what this means:

- `maxUnavailable: 0` → no existing pods can be removed
- `maxSurge: 0` → no extra pods can be added beyond the current count

If you cannot remove old pods and cannot create new ones, how can the update ever progress?
levels.hint3:
The combination `maxSurge: 0` AND `maxUnavailable: 0` creates a deadlock — the update cannot proceed in either direction. Kubernetes detects this and the rollout stalls indefinitely.

Find `spec.strategy.rollingUpdate.maxSurge` in manifest.yaml. At least one of maxSurge or maxUnavailable must be greater than zero.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

Setting both `maxSurge: 0` and `maxUnavailable: 0` is a logical deadlock:
- Kubernetes cannot scale up (maxSurge: 0 → no extra pods allowed)
- Kubernetes cannot scale down (maxUnavailable: 0 → cannot remove any running pod)
- Therefore nothing can change

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, allow at least one direction of movement:

```yaml
strategy:
  type: RollingUpdate
  rollingUpdate:
    maxSurge: 1         # Can create 1 extra pod to start the update
    maxUnavailable: 0   # Keep all 3 running until new ones are ready
```

## Why this matters

This is a subtle but catastrophic misconfiguration. The Deployment appears healthy (pods are running), but it can never be updated. You might discover this only during an incident when you need to push a hotfix — and nothing happens.

levels.description: StatefulSet db was just created alongside its Service, but all pods are stuck in Pending. There are no obvious resource constraints. Run kubectl get pods -n k8smissions.
levels.hint1:
Pending without resource pressure usually means a volume, scheduler constraint, or configuration problem. Read the pod events:

  kubectl describe pod db-0 -n k8smissions

Scroll to the Events section — what reason does Kubernetes give for not starting the pod?
levels.hint2:
The Events may not be obvious here. Check the StatefulSet's requirement for its governing Service:

  kubectl get statefulset db -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep 'serviceName'

Then check if that Service exists and what type it is:

  kubectl get service db-svc -n k8smissions -o yaml | grep 'clusterIP'
levels.hint3:
A StatefulSet requires its `serviceName` to point to a **headless** Service — one with `clusterIP: None`. Without this, Kubernetes cannot create the stable DNS entries (`db-0.db-svc`) that StatefulSet pods depend on.

The current Service `db-svc` is a regular ClusterIP Service. You need to either modify it to be headless or create a new headless Service and update the StatefulSet's serviceName.
levels.reveal:
## What went wrong

The Service `db-svc` has a `clusterIP` assigned (not `None`). A StatefulSet's `serviceName` must reference a **headless Service** (`clusterIP: None`). Without this, the DNS subdomain that gives each pod its stable identity (`db-0.db-svc.k8smissions.svc.cluster.local`) cannot be created.

## Fix

In manifest.yaml, rename or replace the Service to be headless:

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: db-headless
  namespace: k8smissions
spec:
  clusterIP: None        # This is what makes it headless
  selector:
    app: db
  ports:
  - port: 3306
    targetPort: 3306
```

And update the StatefulSet:

```yaml
spec:
  serviceName: db-headless
```

## Why headless?

A headless Service (`clusterIP: None`) does not load-balance — instead, DNS returns the individual IP of each pod. This is how `db-0.db-headless` resolves to exactly pod db-0 and nothing else. Databases need to address replicas individually, not through a load balancer.
